question.text: What happens to the retirement corpus requirement if the expected years in retirement increase from 20 to 25 years (due to longer life expectancy)?

answer.text: The corpus decreases because the money has more time to grow
The corpus remains the same as it depends only on monthly income needs
The corpus increases because it must sustain withdrawals for 5 additional years
The corpus decreases because inflation has a smaller effect over longer periods

explanation.text: **The Logic:** A longer retirement period (25 years vs 20 years) means the corpus must support withdrawals for 60 additional months. The PV of annuity calculation with more periods yields a larger corpus requirement. **The Trap:** The corpus does not decrease with longer retirement — more years of withdrawals require more money. This is the mathematical manifestation of longevity risk. **Cross-Exam Context:** This reinforces why longevity risk is a critical concern. Every additional year of retirement increases the required corpus. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** When planning, use conservative (higher) life expectancy estimates. It is better to have surplus corpus than to run out of money in old age.
